post: #OTD 1913, the 17th Amendment was ratified, and it changed how Americans choose their senators permanently. Before that day, U. S. senators weren't elected by the people. They were chosen by state legislatures. The Founders designed it deliberately as part of the careful architecture of republican government. The Senate was meant to check popular passion. State legislatures were meant to have a direct voice in federal governance, a key feature of federalism. By the early 20th century, critics argued the system had become a vehicle for corruption. Moneyed interests, they said, could effectively purchase Senate seats by controlling state legislators. The Progressive movement pushed hard for reform, and the 17th Amendment was the result: direct, popular election of senators. It's a fascinating case study in constitutional evolution, and in the ongoing American tension between federalism and democratic accountability, between the Founders' design and the people's demand. BRI's lesson on Congress in the 20th and 21st centuries puts this moment in full context for your students 👇  https://bit.ly/4sjHD9m  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #17thAmendment  #ConstitutionalHistory  #Federalism  #AmericanHistory  #TDiH  #BRI  #AmericaAt250

Theodore Roosevelt had a theory about political extremism. He'd seen it before as a child: fire-eaters in the South, radical abolitionists in the North. And he believed Abraham Lincoln had shown the way forward. Not by splitting the difference, but by becoming what he called "the golden mean." Roosevelt spent his entire political career trying to be that. In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, historian David Brown, author of In the Arena: Theodore Roosevelt in War, Peace, and Revolution, joins BRIs Tony Williams to explore how TR's sense of civic duty, his belief in the strenuous life, and his willingness to take on his own party transformed the American presidency. Watch the full conversation:  https://bit.ly/4cwKlUh  #AmericanHistory  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies

Most teachers who share the MyImpact Challenge with their students don't realize there's also something in it for them. The MyImpact Challenge includes a dedicated teacher prize structure. Educators whose students submit valid entries are eligible for recognition, including: - $500 Teacher Grand Prize awarded to the teacher of the national grand prize winner - $500 Teacher Support Prizes for the educators who generate the most entries - $250 Teacher Submission Prize for teachers with 3+ valid submissions from their students The teachers who open this door for their students deserve to be recognized for doing so. The MyImpact Challenge functions best not as an extracurricular add-on, but as a framework that gives structure to the community work students are often already doing. AP Government teachers have used it as an applied civics unit. Service learning coordinators have built whole semesters around it. Guidance counselors have pointed student clubs toward it as a ready-made submission vehicle for projects already underway. In each of those cases, a teacher made it possible. Deadline: May 3. If you have students doing meaningful work in their communities, your next step is straightforward. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #TeacherRecognition  #ServiceLearning  #BillOfRightsInstitute

The Civic Star Challenge is asking you to capture that moment. Share it, enter the weekly $300 drawing, and let the work happening in your classroom count for more than the grade book. #OTD 1968, Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee. He had spent years doing something that history teachers know is harder than it sounds: holding America accountable to its own Founding promises. The Declaration of Independence says all people are created equal, endowed with inalienable rights. King's life's work was a demand that the nation mean it not just as an aspiration, but as an obligation. He didn't deal in abstractions. He marched. He organized. He went to jail. And through it all, he modeled the civic virtues that belong at the center of any serious civics curriculum: courage in the face of real danger, justice as a demand rather than a platitude, humility before the weight of history. At the Bill of Rights Institute, we believe in teaching the whole story of America, including the moments when the nation fell catastrophically short of its ideals. April 4, 1968 is one of those moments. King's legacy – his conviction that the arc of the moral universe bends toward justice – is a lesson in what it looks like to keep believing in a republic when it fails you. Explore BRI's lesson on MLK's dream and identity with your students 👇  https://bit.ly/48dD49n  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #MLK  #MartinLutherKingJr  #CivilRights  #AmericanHistory  #TDiH  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #AmericaAt250

Rosa Parks didn't just want a seat on a bus. She was making a claim on the promises of the Declaration of Independence. BRI's newest A250 Mini Documentary examines the Montgomery Bus Boycott not just as a civil rights milestone but also as the story of ordinary people invoking constitutional principles to demand equal protection under the law. From a single act of defiance to a Supreme Court ruling, this is what civic courage looks like in practice. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4m7qAWH  #RosaParks  #AmericanHistory  #CivilRights  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies  #A250

The MyImpact Challenge deadline is May 3, 2026. For teachers: if you have students who've been working on a community project this year – a service initiative, a class-wide effort, a student business, anything real that started after May 18, 2025 – now is the time to get it submitted. The process is straightforward. Students document what they've done, connect it to the values that guided them, and submit through the online portal. Groups of up to 5 students can enter together. BRI is with you every step of the way. There's $40,000 in total prizes, including a $10,000 grand prize for the top student project in the country, and up to $500 in teacher prizes for educators whose students enter. Every valid submission earns recognition. You've watched your students do the work. Don't let this be the year they made a real difference and nobody outside your classroom ever knew about it. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #ServiceLearning  #BillOfRightsInstitute

American women seized the equality principle in the Declaration of Independence and won the right to vote. But winning the vote didn't settle the question of equality. It opened a harder one. Throughout this series, we've traced that question from Mary Wollstonecraft's 1792 argument that women are rights-bearers, through the founding mothers, the suffragists, the 15th Amendment fracture, Ida B. Wells at the 1913 parade, and the Progressive Era reformers who asked what the vote was actually for. The final episode lands on the debate that still doesn't have a clean answer. Alice Paul: equality requires identical treatment before the law. Florence Kelley: real biological, reproductive, and social differences between men and women mean the law should account for those realities, and laws that do so are equalizing in effect, even if they aren't formally identical. The video offers a concrete test case that makes the stakes clear: should women be required to register for the draft? The Paul answer says yes, equal treatment requires it. The Kelley answer says no, because women bear the primary reproductive burden, different obligations can be equalizing in outcome. No easy resolution. That's the point. 10 episodes. 10 women. 1 question that the Declaration of Independence raised in 1776 and Americans are still working out today: what does equality actually require? The series finale of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4tebBMP  #WomensHistoryMonth  #WomensDeclaration  #Equality  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #America250
